sentences.text: Yeah.
Well, then I-- I-- did I-- did I tell you how I walked out from the ghetto?
Already?
You know, when I-- I got a rake and I walked out, everything?
And I think then, you know, I walk-- I walked till I came to a village.
I walked with other Jewish people.
You know, because they went to work there.
Yeah.
Well, did I tell where the three Ukrainians stopped me?
Yeah.
Well, when they stopped me, they let me go.
And I-- I-- I walked away.
And I walked with a crowd of Jewish people.
They walked to work.
And then I walked away from them.
I came to a village.
And I walked into a farm.
And I ask them if they need somebody to work.
And he said, yes, not for long.
And a-- a Russian [? prison ?] [? of war ?] there.
It was a Polish farm.
And we had to unload, you know, heavy bails of straw, rye.
And I lifted, but it was hard for me to lift it.
And I think I've worked there just one-- I don't know this day, or if-- I forgot if I slept over there or not.
But anyway, after I left there, it was in the morning.
And I went farm to farm, to ask if they need somebody to work.
And I stopped in one-- one farm.
And it was a young girl there.
An-- and I ask if they need somebody to work.
So she said that her mother and father are around the field.
She'll run and she'll ask them if they need somebody, if I want to wait.
So I waited and waited.
And she didn't come.
So I start walking away.
And as soon I walked-- in-- you know, it was bushes there.
And I heard her calling me, you know?
She said, my father wants to talk to you, you know?
He might need somebody to work.
And I went on the field.
And he said, yes, he needs somebody to work.
And I-- I start working there.
And she was a nice lady, her mother.
She asked me what I-- what kind of food I want, she should cook, whatever.
You know?
And I think she only had one girl.
And I-- I-- I slept in the attic.
And one time, I was working on the field.
And our neighbor, a lady, you know, from Rozyszcze, from the ghetto, and she told me, you know, Moshe, they took everything away.
They stole from your house, everything.
I thought what do I care, you know?
I wouldn't go back.
But before the girl, when I start walking away, if she wouldn't have called me, I would walked back to the ghetto, you know?
And-- This was a Polish family you were working for, on the farm?
No, a Ukrainian.
A Ukrainian farm.
Yeah.
They-- I-- I guess they know I were Jewish.
No.
No star--
They didn't ask me nothing, you know?
I just worked there.
No, no.
I didn't hide there.
I just slept there.
You know, they didn't want me--
I really don't know how long I stayed there.
It could be, I think, about two weeks, maybe three weeks.
Maybe a month.
I don't know.
I forgot.
And then one time, you know, I was on the field.
And I was loading the wagon.
And he came.
And he told me, you know, they killed all the Jews in Luck.
You know?
I don't if then were-- they were fighting the Germans.
I-- I really don't know.
I-- I never heard about it.
So it was already on my mind.
And in one week, you know, I think it was on a-- I don't know if it was Sunday or Monday, whatever.
She came up.
I was sleeping on the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --attic, you know, and she told me to leave.
I didn't even see him.
He didn't even come.
The-- the wife, the lady.
The farm lady told me to leave.
But I didn't even see him.
So I walked out.
And they didn't even give me a piece of bread, you know?
And I really worked hard there and everything.
So I walked and I saw a young boy and an old guy, walking.
And I ask him where they're walking, if I can walk with them.
They said no.
They know a farm, you know, where they are going, stay.
So I walked myself.
So I walked to-- I didn't walk well, the way, what people go through.
I just walked through the fields, you know, so nobody will see me.
So I walked, and they just took out from the-- it was the harvest.
They took out the rye, everything.
You know, they put the rye like this.
This through--
I don't-- I didn't know where I was walking to.
I was just walking.
And I heard people talking.
So then I saw girls from our city.
I don't know, 4 or 5 or 6 or 10, I don't know.
They were sitting.
And one boy was sitting there, I knew the boy too.
His name was-- his last name was [INAUDIBLE].
I forgot his first name.
And I was sitting and-- and I didn't eat.
So we took out from the rye, you know, you took it out like this and you put-- you put it in your mouth.
And I don't know if I was in there a day or two days and nights.
And then when we were sitting and talking, I saw a farm.
I came out.
And he cutting the-- the rye or the wheat.
And I went over to him and I ask him if I can help him.
He said, if you don't leave, I'll cut you.
And I have like this.
When he told me this, I came over, you know?
And I looked on the girls.
I looked on the boy and I tell-- ask the boy if he want to go with me as I walked.
He said no.
And the forest, it wasn't a big forest.
It was a small forest.
And I start walking.
And when I was close to the forest, I look back.
And two Ukrainians, they got him.
I think he start walking already.
I saw him walking, I looked.
And then I saw the Ukrainians.
They grabbed him, you know?
And I guess they grabbed the girls, you know?
I guess they killed them, you know?
All the Ukrainians, they killed.
You couldn't trust a Ukrainian farmer.
So this is all-- so for many years, you kept leaving the ghetto and going to work for farmers?
No, they killed already, in the ghetto.
They killed already in the ghetto, when I left the farm.
They killed already.
No, I didn't go back to the ghetto anymore.
No, this was still in 1941.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- I walked into the woods.
And I was walking around.
And a whole family was sitting there.
And it could be it was Friday night and there were [NON-ENGLISH], you know?
It was a father and mother and two boys.
And their grandparents were sitting there.
So I sit down a little with them.
And then I walked away.
And I walked around in the woods, I don't know how long, a month or two months or-- And you were by yourself?
By myself.
To-- to--
To survive, you know, it was-- you know, it was grass.
And-- and it was-- and it was like, little strawberries.
But it was little things I was eating.
But most of that time, I used to go out and ask the farmers for bread.
In the-- in the wintertime, is-- you know, they-- they-- well, it wasn't winter yet.
And-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I walked out there-- and-- and there was so many, you know, Jewish boys and girls and some older ones.
They went out to buy bread and everything.
And I don't think I could get bread there.
Because there was too many of us.
You know?
So I walked back into the woods, to the forest.
And I walked-- walked in the forest, walked in the forest.
And a-- a lot of times, you know, I used to get out, you know, near the forest.
It was turnips, beats, and-- and-- and some other things.
And-- and it was still in the field, what I used to get out the kernels and I used to eat.
But I went into a forest.
And it was a big forest.
And-- and I heard somebody talking.
And I walked.
And it was the whole family, there.
They were sitting.
And you know, they had lice.
You know, they were-- I had lice too.
So I sit down.
Maybe it was in October.
You know?
And-- and we were talking.
And all of a sudden, you know, the-- the Ukrainians, you know, they-- they [?
[UKRAINIAN], ?] [? halt. ?] And it was-- must have been three Ukrainians.
And while they sat there, I start running.
And I left those shoes.
You know, I left the shoes.
And I think I had some other stuff.
And they start shooting at-- and then I thought maybe they wouldn't kill them.
And I start walking back.
But they start shooting.
And I run.
I-- I-- I run away.
And I was running around in the woods.
I don't know what I was eating, or what.
But then it got real, real cold.
Must be November.
And I-- I-- I was just walking around barefooted.
And I had a shirt on.
And I had a-- a jacket.
But it didn't have the sleeves.
And I walked out-- out from the woods.
And I walked.
And it was a farmer, you know?
He was picking up hay or-- from the-- and I asked him-- it was him and it was a Russian prisoner.
He worked for-- for the farm.
I ask him, where shall I go?
I-- I saw houses on the right.
And then I saw houses on the left hand.
And he-- he told me, don't go there, in Ukrainian, but go there.
And I went-- and I went there.
And after I found out the farmer, he was the head of the villages.
You know, the Ukrainian.
And I-- I-- I walked and it was a way, you know, like seven mile.
One, you couldn't go.
And there was a house there.
And I went into the house.
I forget if I knocked on the door or not.
And I asked for bread, if I can stay.
He said, he doesn't have no bread, they don't have.
But I can stay there.
So I went out.
I went to look for-- for bread.
It was a Polish village.
It was no-- maybe there was one Ukrainian living there.
They gave me bread.
And I came back.
He was praying so loud.
So I was afraid.
I start running.
[LAUGHS] But then I-- I came back.
I walk back already.
And I didn't hear him pray.
I walked in.
And a lady, I guess she showed me to walk in.
So she looked through the window.
And she was crying.
A lady, a Polish lady.
And I stayed there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I slept on the attic.
And one time, I was so sick.
I-- I must have eaten-- every time she came up, the lady, and she asked, called me Moishe, How are you feeling?
How are you feeling?
How are you feeling?
You know, and I said, OK.
Yeah, I stayed there.
And after, I came-- I went to look back in the woods, to find a place where I would stay.
And-- and I found a-- the place.
And I-- and I saw, you know, the ground was still fresh.
They killed them, I guess, and they buried them.
But the two boys, you know, they were hiding in a place.
I knew where, by a farmer.
I-- so I stayed-- I stayed there.
His name was Ciechanowiecki.
He was Polish and she was a Russian.
Her name was Olga.
One eye was big, one eye was a little-- real, real, small.
But they were real nice.
He was talking, you know, about the Jews, [? this ?] [? they ?] [? called-- ?] the Jews killed Jesus, and they took him-- took Peter, and they took Jesus there, you know?
They killed him.
And I was just listening, you know?
And-- So you hid out for many months?
There, yeah.
Yeah.
And one time, you know, she came up there, Olga.
And she told me, it's a boy.
And he's asking, he wants to stay.
Shall I let him in or not?
I said, yeah.
And-- and he came up on the attic, you know, and [? he's-- that was ?] [?
Hershel. ?] [?
Hershel? ?]
Yeah.
And he was there.
I forgot.
I forgot his last name, you know?
So well, then in daytime, we had to go into the woods.
And at night, we came back.
But I-- I-- I forgot to tell yo-- this was before [?
Hershel. ?] I went out one time.
I-- I had to go-- in the morning, I-- I had to go into the woods.
At night, I came there, always.
You know?
So one time, I got out from the woods.
And a guy said to me, Moishe, Moishe, come here.
Don't be afraid.
He talked to me in Jewish.
But he wasn't Jewish.
He was a Polish guy.
And he asked me, you know, he said this girl, you know, she is hiding in the farm.
And if I know her, if I can bring her to him.
What I said, I don't know nothing.
This was her, you know, the picture.
On this-- and--
Till 1944.
It was going on for 3 years, 3 years or 2 and 1/2 year.
Well, well, I didn't hear nothing.
I didn't hear-- I didn't hear nothing.
But it was-- it was just one-- one-- one time, you know, two sisters came there.
I'm going to make it short now.
Two sisters came there to this guy.
He didn't have no farm.
He didn't have nothing.
He just had a cow and two pigs.
You know?
And me and the Hershel, in the wintertime, we were hiding, you know, it was-- where they used to keep potatoes in the house.
And they used to cook on top.
At night, we used to crawl in and down there.
I don't know if it was Ukrainian or-- or whatever.
You know, they came-- they came in.
And the ladies, it was two-- two sisters he took in and another lady and two kids, you know, after.
And and what-- well, before they come in, it's real long.
It will take long, you know?
But what is it-- what do you want to tell me?
What is the point?
About how you were hidden?
Yeah.
No, two-- two sisters.
And they had a little boy with them.
You know?
And he took them in too, you know?
I don't know.
I don't-- they knew the farmer.
We didn't know the farmer.
He was not farm-- he just had a house.
He was like a tradesman.
He used to fix shoes.
He used to fix anything.
And then--
Ciechanowiecki.
Ciechanowiecki is the name.
And her name was Olga.
You know?
So--
Yeah.
We slept in the house.
You know, on-- if somebody used to walk in, we used to crawl in and hide.
And--
Yeah, I was afraid.
I was afraid.
And we used to walk around in the-- in the village.
Just one guy, he was the head of the village.
He used to walk through and look at us.
I used to say hello.
He used to shake with a-- you know?
And some, the young boys, they knew us.
They knew we were Jews.
You know?
And the name of this village was?
Ulaniki.
Ulaniki was the name of the village.
From Rozyszcze, 5-- maybe about 8, 10 miles from Rozyszcze.
Well-- oh, you want to-- you want me to jump to, from there?
Oh, yes, not-- yeah.
Yeah.
Well, after-- before the ladies came, a couple came over.
And-- and it-- it would take too long.
Yeah
OK, Go on. yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Many months.
But what happened, I think this was in 1942, already.
But another couple came over.
And-- and this was before the ladies came.
But I'm going to have to skip it, because it will take too long.
In-- in this point, just how to survive in everything.
But in this-- in this point, I have to tell you, is this Ciechanowiecki had a brother.
And he was bandits, Ukrainian bandits.
They killed Jews.
And after, they start killing Polish people.
Because they made Polish police.
The Germans made Polish police.
See, just because it's complicated.
You mean, the-- the bandits?
I saw them when I was in the-- in the forest.
I saw them walking through with rifles.
You know?
But if they would have seen me, they would have killed me, you know?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So they killed his brother, this Ciechanowiecki's brother, where we were staying.
And a lady came and told him-- told this Ciechanowiecki they killed his brother.
They should go and bury him.
They just left him there, the brother and his wife.
So they went to bury this brother.
But I heard the other Polacks tell us is-- she-- she start yelling in the Ukrainian why they-- they-- on the bandits, why they killed their brother.
So they killed them.
They killed the people, what we stay there, you know?
You didn't see this, though?
But you know this happened.
Yeah.
I heard that happen.
You know, they didn't come back.
You know, they didn't come back, no, [? none, either. ?]
No.
None of them came back.
We were in the house.
We and the [?
Hershel ?] and the two sisters, and the son, another-- and another lady.
We didn't stay.
You know, the-- the Polacks, their neighbor, you know, they came right in.
And they took the cow.
They had two little pigs.
They killed it already.
It's so long.
You know, like we went out.
They were cooking.
We were eating their food.
And they-- they-- she was sitting to warm herself.
She said, who's eating my food?
The cat eating my food?
You know, we had lice.
You know, she-- she made the oven, and she put the clothes in to kill the lice, everything.
I didn't take a bath.
I never took a haircut.
You know, my hair were almost like this.
And-- and-- and before they killed them, you know, they killed this Polack, you know, what he talked Jewish.
You know?
But what did you do?
Where did yo go next?
You said you didn't stay there.
Well, at night-- you know, at night, when it came night, we-- we went in, you know, to bushes, me and Hershel, and the ladies, everything.
And we waited till maybe one o'clock, you know?
And then we started walking.
They were closer to the city already.
And--
Rozyszcze.
But it was still maybe about 10 miles.
But from [INAUDIBLE] and I went to-- and on the way, we decided, you know, we separated.
From [?
Hershel, ?] and from all of them.
And--
Because I don't know if we could survive, you know, both.
I don't know.
See, it's a long story.
You know, one time, I-- I hit him, I blood his nose, you know?
And maybe on account of this, and we had-- we had to crawl into another house.
And he could lift me to crawl in through the window.
And I lifted him, you know?
It's-- it's a big [? story. ?] So we divided.
And-- Did you think your chances of surviving were better if you were alone?
I think-- I think it was better.
I don't know if we-- maybe we wouldn't have survived, both of us.
And I--
Well--
